entry.title: ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, S
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, Scalably, and Cleanly

1.      Introduction

Utilities are accelerating their digital transformation, but one architectural frontier remains notoriously difficult to conquer:

How do you safely ingest high-frequency Operational Technology (OT) data from substations, feeders, and Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) into SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) without introducing cyber vulnerabilities or disrupting grid reliability?

Legacy architecture often relied on brittle, custom-coded point-to-point connections or heavy middleware that violated basic security isolation principles. This article provides a comprehensive, production-ready, architecture-driven blueprint for securely integrating:

  • IEC 61850 substation telemetry (GOOSE/MMS)
  • MQTT/Sparkplug B telemetry streams
  • SCADA / RTU / IED asynchronous events
  • Grid-edge IoT sensors

We will achieve this by leverage modern event-driven architectures (EDA), Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA), and SAP Clean Core design patterns.

2.      Why OT-to-IT Integration Challenges Enterprise

Bridging the physical grid and the digital cloud requires reconciling two fundamentally opposing engineering philosophies:

Dimension

Operational Technology (OT)

Enterprise Information Technology (IT)

Primary Priority

Availability & Safety: Human life and grid stability. Zero downtime tolerated.

Confidentiality & Integrity: Data security, compliance, and financial accuracy.

Protocols

Industrial, low-overhead, non-IP, or deterministic (IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus).

Web-native, human-readable, RESTful (HTTPS, OData, gRPC).

Network Philosophy

Air-gapped, strictly segmented (Purdue Model Level 0-3), highly localized.

Cloud-native, perimeterless, distributed, global access.

Data Characteristics

Ultra-high frequency (milliseconds), time-series bursts, massive velocity.

Transactional, structured, stateful, request-response.

To bridge this gap safely, cloud ingestion must be completely outbound-only from the lower levels, strictly audited, cryptographically verified, and decoupled via an enterprise event broker.

3.      Business impact on Utilities

This architecture is not just a technical modernization—it directly enables:

  • Reduced unplanned outages through real-time anomaly detection
  • Lower maintenance costs via predictive asset health insights
  • Improved regulatory compliance with secure audit-ready telemetry flows
  • Faster field response through automated S/4HANA work order generation
  • Scalable grid-edge onboarding without re-platforming legacy OT systems

4.      Reference Architecture: Purdue Model to SAP BTP

The architecture below maps the telemetry flow across the classic Purdue Model for Industrial Control Systems, ensuring that no direct connection is ever established between the cloud and Level 2/3 critical infrastructure.

5.      Architectural Safeguards Highlighted:

  • Directional Isolation: Firewalls block all inbound traffic to the Substation OT network. The Industrial Edge Gateway establishes an outbound connection to the Upper DMZ broker.
  • Decoupling Layer: SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) acts as the shock absorber, ensuring that high-frequency grid telemetry bursts do not overwhelm downstream ERP APIs.

*Architectural Principle: Direct OT-to-cloud integrations without DMZ brokers are inherently unsafe.  Any architecture that bypasses the industrial DMZ violates fundamental Purdue Model isolation and must be avoided.

6.      Deep-Dive: Protocol Translation & Topic Design

 The Conversion: IEC 61850 to Lightweight Cloud Events: IEC 61850 uses Manufacturing Message Specification (MMS) for complex reporting and Generic Object-Oriented Substation Events (GOOSE) for peer-to-peer microsecond trips. These cannot be parsed by cloud apps directly.

An Edge Gateway maps these complex hierarchical Logical Nodes (e.g., MMXU1 for Three-Phase Voltage/Current) into standard JSON structures wrapped in the CloudEvents specification.

Production-Grade Ingestion Payload Example

Below is the exact schema passed from the Upper DMZ to SAP Advanced Event Mesh when a transformer experiences a thermal anomaly:

JSON { "specversion": "1.0",

  "id": "evt-773a-4b92-88ef-22114949ad45", "source": "/us-east/substation-s12/transformer-tx03",   "type":

"com.utility.grid.telemetry.v1",

  "datacontenttype": "application/json",

  "time": "2026-06-01T18:21:55.003Z",

  "data": { "logical_node": "YPTR1",

    "component": "WindingTemperature",

    "metrics": {  "temperature_celsius": 98.4,

      "ambient_celsius": 32.1,

      "cooling_fan_status": "ON_MAX" },

    "status": { "health_score": 72.5,

      "anomaly_detected": true,

      "severity": "WARNING" } } }

Enterprise Topic Hierarchy Best Practices

To optimize routing efficiency within SAP Advanced Event Mesh and avoid bloated subscription wildcards, we enforce a structured, predictable topic hierarchy layout:

$$\text{Domain} \,/\, \text{Business Unit} \,/\, \text{Geographic Zone} \,/\, \text{Substation ID} \,/\, \text{Asset Category} \,/\, \text{Asset ID} \,/\, \text{Event Type}$$

Examples:

  • grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry
  • grid/transmission/midwest/sub-s45/circuit-breaker/cb07/state

This allows BTP consumers to subscribe with surgical precision. For instance, a predictive maintenance engine can listen to all transformer telemetry across the entire Northeast region using the pattern: grid/distribution/northeast/+/transformer/+/telemetry.

7.      The Blueprint in Action: A Practitioner Scenario

To understand how this architecture operates under pressure, let's trace a real-world grid event: A severe thermal anomaly on Substation Transformer TX03 located in the Northeast distribution zone.

Step 1: The Edge Trigger (Purdue Level 2)

  1. The physical temperature sensor on Transformer TX03 detects a sudden spike, hitting $98.4^\circ\text{C}$.
  2. The local Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) broadcasts this telemetry via an IEC 61850 MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification) reporting block over the substation's internal fiber optic bus.

Step 2: Protocol Translation & Hardening (Industrial DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The OT Industrial Edge Gateway (running Litmus Edge) intercepts the raw MMS packet.
  2. It strips out the heavy industrial headers, extracts the data fields from the Logical Node YPTR1 (the standard IEC 61850 node for a power transformer), and maps them into a standardized, web-friendly JSON payload.
  3. The gateway packages this payload inside a standardized CloudEvents v1.0 wrapper.

Step 3: Secure Egress to the DMZ Proxy (Enterprise DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The Edge Gateway initiates an outbound-only TLS 1.3 connection to the Upper DMZ MQTT Broker (HiveMQ Cluster).
  2. It passes hardware-secured mTLS client certificates to prove its identity and publishes the message to the strict, isolated topic:

 “grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry”

Step 4: Ingestion by SAP Advanced Event Mesh (SAP BTP Layer)

  1. The HiveMQ cluster securely routes the incoming stream over a secure WebSocket connection into SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via Dynamic Message Routing (DMR).
  2. Because AEM natively understands the hierarchical topic layout, it instantly clones and routes the message to three concurrent, decoupled subscribers without any database lag:
    • SAP Datasphere Ingestion Queue: For long-term historical time-series storage and grid analytics.
    • SAP AI Core Engine: To recalculate the transformer's Remaining Useful Life (RUL) score.
    • CAP Event Processor Queue: Dedicated to immediate, critical rule evaluation.

Step 5: Real-Time Orchestration & Clean Core Action

  1. A lightweight SAP CAP (Cloud Application Model) microservice consumes the message from its dedicated AEM queue.
  2. The service executes an automated rule: If temperature_celsius > $95^\circ\text{C}$ and cooling_fan_status == "ON_MAX", trigger emergency field intervention.
  3. Rather than writing complex custom ABAP tables inside the ERP, the CAP service uses the standard SAP Integration Suite to call the out-of-the-box S/4HANA OData v4 API (API_EQUIPMENT_SRV).
  4. The Result: An automated Urgent Corrective Maintenance Work Order is instantly generated in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management with a high-priority dispatch flag—all accomplished within 2.3 seconds of the physical temperature spike, leaving the digital core clean, secure, and upgrade-ready.

8.      Advanced Substation-to-BTP Integration Patterns

 *Reality Check*: Batch ingestion architectures are fundamentally incompatible with modern grid telemetry. Millisecond-level data streams require real-time, event-driven processing models—not delayed batch pipelines.

Depending on your edge compute availability and governance requirements, choose one of these production deployment topologies:

Pattern A: Multi-Tiered Message Broker Architecture (Recommended for Zero-Trust)

  • Mechanism: An Edge Gateway pushes local data to an on-premises DMZ MQTT cluster (e.g., Hive MQ). Secure bridge configuration clones and forwards permitted topics out to SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via cloud connectors or secure WebSocket’s.
  • Pros: Strict isolation; if the cloud connection drops, the local DMZ broker caches events, mitigating data loss.

Pattern B: Intelligent Edge Compute with Stream Processing (Recommended for High Frequency)

  • Mechanism: Deploy an industrial edge compute node (running lightweight runtimes like Node-RED or KubeEdge) physically inside the substation. It aggregates millisecond telemetry, calculates rolling 1-minute means/standard deviations, and publishes only summarized statistical health events to SAP BTP.
  • Pros: Radically reduces egress data costs and avoids overwhelming BTP with trivial data points.

9.      Security & Governance Blueprint (Non-Negotiable Controls)

Bringing OT data to the cloud is impossible without an airtight security architecture. Implement the following parameters at each layer:

  • Mutual TLS (mTLS) with Hardware Roots of Trust: Every edge gateway must authenticate against the DMZ broker using X.509 client certificates issued by an internal Utility Certificate Authority (CA). Private keys must be stored in secure TPM 2.0 hardware chips on the edge device.
  • Granular Access Control Lists (ACLs): Edge gateways must be explicitly barred from publishing to topics outside their assigned asset scope. A compromise of Substation A must never allow injection of malicious events into Substation B.
  • Schema Validation at the Ingress Gate: Use the Schema Validation features in SAP Advanced Event Mesh. If a message arrives containing bad structures, malformed types, or executable code payloads, it is immediately shunted to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for forensics, blocking code injection vectors.
  • Network Partitioning & Backpressure Controls: If the connection between the utility DMZ and SAP BTP degrades, the DMZ broker must utilize Local Storage Buffering. High QoS (Quality of Service) 1 or 2 settings must be applied strictly to critical operational state changes (e.g., breaker trips) to guarantee delivery, while standard continuous metrics use QoS 0 to save bandwidth.

10. Realizing Enterprise Value in SAP BTP

Once normalized grid events stream cleanly into SAP Advanced Event Mesh, they become actionable assets without touching the digital core directly:

*Strategic Insight*: Event Mesh is not optional—it is the control plane for grid-scale ingestion.  Without event-driven decoupling, high-frequency OT telemetry cannot be safely or scalable integrated into enterprise systems.

The Event Consumer Microservices Layer

Deploy lightweight, stateless microservices within the SAP BTP Kyma Runtime or SAP Cloud Application Model (CAP) to process incoming streams:

 

 

 

  • Real-Time Grid Insights: Kyma functions scan the event properties. If a transformer temperature exceeds defined thresholds (temperature_celsius > 95), an immediate event is triggered.
  • AI-Driven Asset Health Optimization: Data streams scale out directly into SAP Datasphere and SAP AI Core. Machine Learning models calculate remaining useful life (RUL) metrics based on historical load/thermal characteristics, sending updated health indices back to operational dashboards.

The Clean Core Imperative

By processing events asynchronously in BTP, the core enterprise system (SAP S/4HANA / IS-U) remains entirely untouched by heavy custom code. S/4HANA only participates when action is required—such as creating an asset maintenance order or executing a field service dispatch via standard standard OData v4 APIs. The system updates and upgrades effortlessly, unencumbered by massive custom databases full of RAW time-series data.

11. End-to-End Flow: Transformer Overheating Scenario

  • A transformer in Substation S12 exceeds thermal threshold (98.4°C).
  • IEC 61850 MMS reports are captured by Edge Gateway.
  • Gateway converts payload to Cloud Events JSON.
  • Event is securely published (mTLS) to DMZ MQTT broker.
  • Broker forwards allowed topics to SAP Advanced Event Mesh.
  • AEM routes the event to a CAP microservice.
  • CAP evaluates rules → triggers anomaly event.
  • SAP AI Core enriches with predictive health score.
  • SAP S/4HANA automatically creates a maintenance work order.
  • Field service team is dispatched proactively.

12. Operational Summary Blueprint

  1. The convergence of OT and IT is no longer optional—it is foundational to modern utilities.
  2. However, success depends not on connectivity alone, but on *how* that connectivity is governed. 
  3. Architectures that prioritize strict isolation, event-driven decoupling, and Clean Core principles will define the next generation of grid intelligence.
  4. SAP BTP, when used correctly, becomes not just an integration platform—but the control tower for real-time, autonomous utility operations.

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Activation on SAP BTP and Database Operations Using JDBC from Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on integration scenarios in SAP BTP, there are many cases where SAP Cloud Integration needs to directly read from or write to a database. SAP HANA Cloud is the obvious choice on BTP, and the JDBC adapter in Cloud Integration makes this integration straightforward—once the setup is clear.

In this blog, I will walk through:

  • How to activate SAP HANA Cloud in SAP BTP
  • How to create a simple database structure (schema, table, user)
  • How to connect SAP Cloud Integration to HANA Cloud using JDBC
  • How to perform basic SELECT, INSERT, and UPDATE operations from an iFlow

The focus of this blog is mainly on the enablement and configuration steps, because that is where most issues usually occur.

You can read my previous blogs here on SAP TMS and SAP ANS Integration with Cloud Integration

Content Transport Using SAP Cloud Transport Management Service for SAP CI CF Environment

Setting Up Alerts for SAP Cloud Integration Using SAP Alert Notification Service 

 

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

  • Access to an SAP BTP Global Account
  • A Subaccount (Cloud Foundry environment)
  • SAP Integration Suite enabled
  • Required roles to:
    • Create HANA Cloud instances
    • Access HANA Database Explorer
    • Configure Cloud Integration

Step 1: Enable SAP HANA Cloud in SAP BTP

Check Entitlements

First, ensure SAP HANA Cloud is entitled:

  1. Go to BTP Cockpit ->Global Account ->Entitlements

Check if SAP HANA Cloud is available and assign quota to your subaccount if needed

If the entitlement is missing, you won’t be able to create a HANA Cloud instance.

Subscribe SAP HANA Cloud Tools application

  1. Navigate to Subaccount -> Instances and Subscriptions ->Create

Select Service: SAP HANA Cloud and Plan: tools

 

Create SAP HANA Cloud Instance

  1. Navigate to Subaccount ->Instances and Subscriptions->Create

Select Service: SAP HANA Cloud and Plan: hana
Provide Instance name (example: hana-cloud-ci-db) and System password

Create the instance , provisioning usually takes 20–30 minutes, so be patient here.

Verify Database Status

Once the instance is created:

Assign below roles to your user to open and perform operations in HANA Cloud 

Navigate to Subaccount ->Security->Users ->Assign Roles

  1. Open SAP HANA Cloud from the subaccount 
Make sure the database status is Running

Step 2: Create Database Objects

Open HANA Database Explorer

  1. From HANA Cloud Central->Open Database Explorer
Log in using the admin user (DBADMIN) 
\
Select SQL Console

Create Schema and Table

Create a schema first:

CREATE SCHEMA CI_DEMO_SCHEMA;

Now create a simple table:

CREATE TABLE CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE (

    EMP_ID INTEGER NOT NULL,

    EMP_NAME NVARCHAR(100),

    DEPARTMENT NVARCHAR(50),

    SALARY DECIMAL(15, 2),

    PRIMARY KEY (EMP_ID)

);

 

Insert some test data:

INSERT INTO CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE

VALUES (1, 'ABC DEF', 'IT', 90000);

 

Create a Technical User for Integration
Do not use DBADMIN for integration access.

CREATE USER CI_JDBC_USER PASSWORD "Pas****@1**3";
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON SCHEMA CI_DEMO_SCHEMA TO CI_JDBC_USER;

This user will be used later in Cloud Integration.

Step 3: Configure JDBC Connectivity in Cloud Integration

Get JDBC Connection Details

From HANA Cloud Central, note the host by Copy SQL Endpoint

98dd5c42*****************0d8792.hna1.prod-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443

HANA Cloud JDBC URL format required  as per below :

jdbc:sap://<host>:443/?encrypt=true and Port 443 is used with SSL.

The complete URL will be become as per below :

jdbc:sap://98dd5c42*****************0d8792.hna1.prod-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443/?encrypt=true

Navigate to Integration Suite->Security ->Manage JDBC Data Source and maintain as per below 

Configure JDBC Adapter in iFlow

HTTPS Sender to Trigger message from Postman and JDBC Receiver 

In JDBC connection maintain the JDBC Data Source name created in previous step.

Step 4: Testing

1. Select Operation 

Configure the Postman to trigger CI iflow to trigger the HTTP endpoint

SELECT * FROM CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE;
 

2. Insert Operation

<root>
    <insert_statement>
        <dbTableName action="INSERT">
            <table>CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE</table>
            <access>
                <EMP_ID>2</EMP_ID>
                <EMP_NAME>GGG HHHH</EMP_NAME>
                <DEPARTMENT>SAP</DEPARTMENT>
                <SALARY>99000.00</SALARY>
            </access>
        </dbTableName>
    </insert_statement>
</root>

 

Perform select again to view the inserted record 

3. Update operation :

<root>
    <update_statement>
        <dbTableName action="UPDATE">
            <table>CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE</table>
            <access>
                <EMP_NAME>NNN JJJ</EMP_NAME>
            </access>
            <key>
                <EMP_ID>2</EMP_ID>
            </key>
        </dbTableName>
    </update_statement>
</root>


Perform select again to view updated record 
Table from HABA Cloud DB 

Summary 

This POC highlighted several common pitfalls when working with SAP HANA Cloud and the JDBC adapter in SAP Cloud Integration. Most issues were not related to connectivity, but rather to user security policies, JDBC execution modes, and strict XML structure requirements. Understanding these early helps avoid hours of debugging.

Reference 

JDBC Receiver Adapter Payload Operations

SAP HANA Cloud

entry.title: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite is a powerful platform for building and operating integrations at enterprise scale. Its design environment is feature-rich, its runtime is reliable, and its API coverage is broad. But for teams managing dozens — or hundreds — of integration flows across multiple environments, the operational side of the story can quickly become the bottleneck.

Consider a typical scenario: a new release is ready. The team needs to promote integration packages from development to test and then to production. Each package needs its externalized parameters re-applied for the target environment. Log levels need to be set appropriately. Each flow needs to be triggered for activation. If any object is locked, or if the deployment order is wrong, the team has to intervene manually. And when a flow is retired, there is no mechanism to transport that deletion — someone has to remove it by hand in each environment.

The same challenge applies to Partner Directory entries — a critical configuration object for many integration scenarios, especially pipeline-based architectures. Partner Directory entries have no built-in transport mechanism. Setting them up manually on a production system, under time pressure, with no rollback option, is a risk that teams simply should not have to accept.

None of this is a criticism of the platform. It simply reflects what happens when integration teams grow, release frequencies increase, and the margin for error shrinks. Manual processes that work fine for small teams become fragile at scale.

This is the problem that CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite was designed to solve.

What Is It?

CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite is an open-source CI/CD automation layer for SAP Integration Suite, built on GitHub Actions. It was developed jointly by SAP and Mercedes-Benz, born from real operational needs on a large-scale integration landscape. It has been running in production since October 2025 and is available today under the Apache 2.0 license:

https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

A quick note on GitHub Actions — if you are not familiar with it: GitHub Actions is the built-in automation platform of GitHub. It lets you define workflows that run in response to events (a button click, a schedule, or a code push). No separate CI/CD tool to install. The entire deployment pipeline runs directly inside your GitHub repository.

The Core Idea: Git as the Single Source of Truth

The solution is built on one principle: Git holds everything — not just the integration flow source code, but also the configuration that goes with it: externalized parameter values per environment, deployment configuration per flow, Access Policies, and Partner Directory content.

GitHub Actions then uses this Git-stored configuration to fully automate the path from development to production. Deletions travel through the pipeline. Parameter changes are applied automatically. Log levels are managed after deployment. No manual intervention between environments.

SAP Integration Suite — DEV
     │  Download package as ZIP
     │  Extract + merge configuration
     ▼
Git Repository
     │  Change detection
     │  Environment-specific parameters stored as code
     ▼
SAP Integration Suite — TST / PRD / ...
     │  Upload to Design Time
     │  Activate to Runtime
     │  Apply environment-specific log levels
     ▼
Done — fully automated, fully auditable

Every step is recorded in Git. Every deployment is traceable. Every configuration change is reviewed before it is applied.

The Workflow Library at a Glance

The solution ships as a library of reusable GitHub Actions workflows and composite actions. Teams adopt it through a single sync workflow that pulls the latest templates automatically — when new features are released, your workflows update themselves.

 
Environment dropdowns are populated automatically from your configured GitHub Environments. DEV and TST are required with those exact names; any further stages — such as a pre-production or production environment — can be named freely.

Workflow What It Does

Download → Git → DeployDownload an Integration Package or Partner Directory from DEV, store it in Git, deploy to TST
Deploy / DeleteUpload or delete Integration Packages and Partner Directory entries to any configured environment
Delete from BTP and GitRemove an Integration Package or Partner Directory from all environments and from Git
Download to GitSnapshot Integration Packages or Partner Directory entries from any environment to a Git branch
Release ImportCalculate the delta between two Git refs and deploy changed packages to a target environment
Update iFlow ParametersApply externalized parameter changes to a specific iFlow and optionally redeploy
Access Policies (3 workflows)Download, upload, and manage Access Policies across environments
Dashboard GeneratorGenerate the operations dashboard
Create ReleaseVersioned release management from the development branch
 

Every workflow that operates on Integration Packages also supports Partner Directory entries — selected via a single mode input.

What's New in v1.3.0

Version 1.3.0 is the largest release to date.

The Dashboard — See Your Entire Landscape at a Glance

The headline feature is the Integration Suite Operations Dashboard: a static HTML page, automatically generated by a scheduled workflow and published to GitHub Pages. It shows every integration package and every IFlow across all configured environments — runtime status, deployed version, version drift between stages, and whether the package is managed by the pipeline. Color-coded health indicators make problem areas immediately visible.

The dashboard gives an immediate overview of every integration package across all environments — version drift, deployment errors, and unmanaged packages are visible at a glance.

Expanding a package reveals the full detail. Two typical situations a team encounters during release work:

Version drift — both IFlows are running a newer version on DEV and TST than on PRD. Typical during an active release: the new version has been promoted to test but not yet to production. Missing deployment — the second IFlow is configured for production but shows — in the PRD runtime column: it has not been imported yet. The deployment configuration says it should be there; BTP says it is not. Without this dashboard, that gap would likely go unnoticed until a runtime error surfaces.

The dashboard is regenerated daily and can be triggered manually at any time. Self-contained HTML — no server required.

GitHub App Authentication

v1.3.0 replaces Personal Access Tokens with GitHub App authentication. Two dedicated apps handle the read and write sides of the pipeline with minimal, explicit permissions. Token rotation is automatic.

Access Policies as Code

Access Policies can now be version-controlled and promoted through the pipeline. The pattern is identical to integration packages: download from DEV, commit to Git, review, deploy.

Automatic Log Level Management

The deployment pipeline now automatically sets each iFlow's log level to the configured operational level after deployment. No manual navigation to the operations cockpit after every release.

A Different Approach to Integration Lifecycle Management

 
SAP provides transport and lifecycle management capabilities for Integration Suite. This solution takes a different path: Git becomes the system of record, GitHub Actions drives the entire lifecycle, and every operation — download, deploy, delete, configure — is automated and auditable. For teams that have adopted a DevOps mindset and want full control over their integration pipeline, this is a deliberate alternative rather than an add-on.

Built Together, Open Sourced Together

This solution was developed collaboratively by SAP and Mercedes-Benz to reduce the manual overhead of release operations on a large enterprise integration landscape. It is available to any team, free of charge, under the Apache 2.0 license, and works on both GitHub Enterprise Cloud and GitHub Enterprise Server.

What's Coming in This Series

  1. The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline — what actually happens, step by step
  2. Managing parameters and log levels — environment-specific configuration as code
  3. The Release Import workflow — controlled promotion using Git tags and branches
  4. Access Policies as code — governance and auditability
  5. The Dashboard in depth — how to read it, configure it, and act on what it shows
  6. Extending the pipeline with Customer Exits — inject your own tests and deployment rules without touching the core

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline
entry.content:
The first post in this series introduced CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite: what it is, why it exists, and what it looks like from the outside. This post goes inside the machine.

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow is the beating heart of the pipeline. It is the first workflow most teams run when they adopt the solution, and it is the one they use every time a developer changes something on DEV and wants to move it forward. Understanding exactly what happens in each step — and why those steps are designed the way they are — is the foundation for understanding everything else in the system.

  • The Problem It Solves
  • The Git Branch Model
  • What You Fill In
  • Inside the Pipeline
    • Authorization and Environment Resolution
    • Download and Commit to Git
    • Partner Directory in Git: Decoded and Readable
    • Clean Slate Before Deployment
    • Upload and Activate
  • What the Pipeline Does Not Do
  • Key Takeaways

The Problem It Solves

In a typical SAP Integration Suite landscape, a developer makes changes on the development tenant. Those changes live in the SAP Integration Suite design time. When it is time to promote them, someone has to manually export the package, import it into the test system, apply the right configuration values, and deploy each artifact — Message Mappings, Script Collections, Value Mappings, and iFlows. If anything was already deployed in test, it might need to be cleaned up first. Parameters might differ between systems. Log levels need attention. And none of it is recorded anywhere in a way that supports auditability or rollback.

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow replaces all of that with a single button press that is fully automated, fully recorded, and repeatable.

The Git Branch Model

Before walking through the pipeline, it helps to understand how Git is structured in this solution. There are two permanent branches with distinct roles.

The development branch is where all active work lives. Every package download, every configuration change, every new artifact lands here first. This is the branch the Download → Git → Deploy workflow writes to, and it is the branch the deployment jobs read from when uploading to TST.

There is no option to select a different target branch when triggering the workflow. The development branch is hardcoded as the destination. This is a deliberate design choice: developers should not have to think about Git branches at all during the day-to-day download and deploy cycle. The branch model is an infrastructure concern, not a developer concern. As landscape scenarios grow more complex — multiple parallel development streams, for example — this may evolve, but for the common case the simplicity is intentional.

The main branch represents what has been formally released. As long as work is progressing on development, main is untouched. The merge from development into main happens as part of the Release Import process — which creates a Git tag, a GitHub Release, and the clean reference point used for controlled promotion to production. That process is covered in a later post in this series.

What You Fill In

The workflow is (Dev) BTP Download to GIT and Upload/Deploy to TST, triggered manually via workflow_dispatch. The team member fills in three fields:

  • ID — the Integration Package ID or Partner Directory partner ID to process
  • Mode — IntegrationPackages or PartnerDirectory
  • JIRA Story / SNOW Incident — a reference that becomes the Git commit message, linking every change in the repository to a traceable work item

One important note on the ID field: the pipeline always operates on complete entities as its smallest unit. In IntegrationPackages mode, every operation applies to the entire Integration Package — there is no option to download or deploy a single iFlow or other individual artifact. In PartnerDirectory mode, the same principle applies: one complete Partner Directory entry per ID. This applies to all workflows in this CICD solution, not just this one.

All artifacts must be versioned — before triggering the workflow, every artifact in the package must have a saved version in SAP Integration Suite. Draft artifacts are not supported and will cause the download to fail. This is a standard practice in any version-controlled pipeline: only content that has been deliberately saved as a version is considered ready to leave the development tenant.

A note on Package IDs — if the ID in the screenshot looks unusual (KIhovby8X0TS8uXS3Oo1CCAcu), that is intentional. When creating a package in SAP Integration Suite, the ID is a free-text field you set once at creation time. Unlike the package name, it can never be changed afterwards. Using a GUID avoids any confusion down the line: names get renamed, reorganised, or reused — a GUID stays unambiguous forever.

Three fields, one button. The ID and Mode select what to process; the JIRA or SNOW reference becomes the Git commit message, tying every repository change to a traceable work item.

Inside the Pipeline

That is it for the inputs. Everything else is automated. The screenshot below shows a completed run for an integration package — 1m 34s from button press to a fully deployed package on TST. The actual runtime depends on the number of artifacts in the package: the deployment phase is typically the longest part of the process, as all artifacts are activated — Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically, while iFlows are deployed based on their individual deployment configuration. The sections below explain what each phase does and why it is designed the way it is.

 

Authorization and Environment Resolution

Before anything touches SAP Integration Suite, the workflow verifies that the person who triggered it is a member of the authorized GitHub team. The team slug is defined in the BTP_DEV_TEAM_SLUG repository variable, configured once during the initial repository setup. If the check fails, the run stops immediately — no SAP Integration Suite credentials are used, no API calls are made. This applies to both modes.

Authorization is not just a security gate. It is also where the workflow binds to the DEV GitHub Environment — a named set of secrets and variables scoped to a specific job — and resolves all the connection details — BTP API URL, token endpoint, technical user, and service key identity — that every subsequent step will use. Before the deployment phase, the workflow binds to the TST GitHub Environment in the same way — resolving its own set of credentials and API endpoints. The team membership check is not repeated: it runs once, at the start of the workflow, and covers the entire run. No URLs or credentials are hardcoded anywhere in the workflow. Everything is environment-driven, which means the same workflow definition works across any landscape configuration without modification — adding a stage is a matter of adding a GitHub Environment with the appropriate credentials, not changing the workflow itself.

Download and Commit to Git

Once authorization is confirmed and the DEV environment is resolved, the pipeline checks out the development branch and removes the existing content for the package or Partner Directory ID. This clean-first approach ensures that if an artifact was deleted on DEV, it does not silently survive in the repository. The pipeline then authenticates with SAP Integration Suite, fetches the new content, and commits it — what lands in Git is an exact reflection of what is in SAP Integration Suite at that moment.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the package is fetched as a ZIP archive. The extraction goes two levels deep: the outer package ZIP is unpacked, and each artifact archive inside it is unpacked as well. The result is that real source files — iFlow configurations, Groovy scripts, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, Script Collections — land as individual readable files in Git. Every change to a Groovy script or a mapping is visible in the commit diff and reviewable in a pull request. It also opens the door for automated code scans — static analysis of Groovy scripts for security or quality requirements — as part of custom pipeline enhancements. And because the source files are plain text, the repository's built-in search becomes a lightweight where-used tool: searching across all packages for a specific endpoint URL, a function name, or a configuration value instantly shows every iFlow and script that references it.

For PartnerDirectory mode, the four object types are retrieved individually via API calls and written as four JSON files, one per type: String Parameters, Binary Parameters, Alternative Partners, and Authorized Users.

Regardless of mode, the commit uses the JIRA or SNOW reference entered at dispatch as the commit message. Every change in the repository is therefore traceable to a work item. Configuration and deployment control files for each iFlow are also created or updated as part of the commit — the detail on these is covered in the next post in this series. Crucially, these files are separate from the artifact content that is deleted and re-fetched on each download; any stage-specific parameter values or deployment settings the team has configured are preserved across repeated runs, not overwritten.

Partner Directory in Git: Decoded and Readable

The way Binary Parameters are stored in Git deserves a special mention. In the Partner Directory of SAP Integration Suite, Binary Parameter values are base64-encoded blobs — not human-readable in the portal. The pipeline stores them in two parts. The index file BinaryParameters.json lists each entry with its metadata and a filename reference instead of the raw encoded value. The actual content is decoded and written as a separate file under a Binary/ subfolder, named after the entry.

 

The Value field holds a filename reference, not a base64 blob. The decoded content lives as a separate file in the Binary/ subfolder.

The result is that an XSD schema used for Receiver Determination, or any other binary payload, is stored as its actual readable content in the repository. The example below is a simplified illustration — in practice this file would contain a full XSD definition.

 

The decoded file as it appears in Git. Every change to the content is visible in the commit diff and reviewable in a pull request — something simply not possible with an opaque encoded string.

Clean Slate Before Deployment

Before uploading the new version to TST, the pipeline clears what is already there. This happens in two layers, and the order matters.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the runtime artifacts are undeployed first. The order matters: if the design-time package were deleted while artifacts are still active in the runtime, a runtime restart would leave those artifacts unable to find their definitions — or worse, leave orphaned objects in the runtime that no longer have any design-time counterpart and that nobody remembers deploying. Cleaning the runtime first avoids that risk. Only once the runtime is clear is the design-time package deleted.

For PartnerDirectory mode, there is no runtime deployment to stop. The existing Partner Directory entries are deleted directly from the design time.

In both cases, clearing the design time completely before uploading is what makes the clean-slate guarantee possible. If an artifact was removed from the package on DEV, it will not survive on TST — there is no mechanism by which a deleted artifact can linger in the target environment. Without this step, TST would gradually accumulate ghost artifacts that no longer exist on DEV, making it an unreliable mirror of the source.

Upload and Activate

With the target tenant clean, the pipeline uploads the new content from the development Git branch and activates it.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the package source is reassembled into a ZIP — the format the SAP Integration Suite public API expects for package upload — and uploaded to the TST design time. The deployment engine then activates the artifacts in the package: Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically. Integration Flows are deployed selectively — each iFlow has a deployment flag in its configuration file committed to Git, and only those marked for deployment in the target environment are activated. Since the package was deleted and re-uploaded, every deployed iFlow is new to the runtime and starts with the default log level of INFO. After deployment, the pipeline updates each iFlow's log level to whatever is configured for that environment in Git. No one needs to visit the operations cockpit to check or adjust anything afterward.

For PartnerDirectory mode, the pipeline creates the Partner Directory entries on the TST Cloud Integration runtime. Support for Edge Integration Cell runtimes is planned and will be added once the required public APIs become available.

What the Pipeline Does Not Do

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow intentionally stops at TST. It does not automatically promote to production. Moving to PRD — or to any further stage — is a deliberate, separate action. The Release Import workflow handles that step, using Git tags and branch comparisons to calculate exactly what has changed since the last release and deploy only the delta. That is the subject of a later post in this series.

Key Takeaways

  • The pipeline operates on complete entities as its smallest unit: one full Integration Package in IntegrationPackages mode, one complete Partner Directory entry in PartnerDirectory mode. Individual iFlows or artifacts cannot be targeted in isolation — this applies to all workflows in the solution.
  • A single workflow dispatch downloads a package from DEV, commits it to Git with a traceable reference, and deploys it to TST — fully automated, no manual handover.
  • The download creates and maintains one configuration file per package containing the parameter values for all iFlows in that package, and one deployment control file per package covering all iFlows — all committed alongside the package source in Git. Stage-specific values set by the team are preserved across repeated downloads. The detail is covered in the next post.
  • The clean-before-upload pattern ensures the target environment reflects Git exactly, including deletions.
  • Partner Directory is fully supported by the same pipeline. Switching the mode input is all it takes — the same authorization, Git commit, and deployment steps apply, with Partner Directory-specific handling where the content differs.
  • Every action that happens — every download, every parameter value, every deployment decision — is recorded in Git history.

The next post in this series covers the configuration layer in depth: how externalized parameters and log levels are managed per environment, how to add a new stage, and how to update a parameter value without triggering a full download.

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: NEW: 🇩🇪 SAP BTP Live Sessions auf Deutsch 🇩🇪
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

am Montag ging’s bei uns mit englischen Sessions los. Und heute legen wir nach. Denn Mittwoch ist doch eigentlich perfekt, um den eigenen Lernplan aufzustellen: Genug vom Wochenstart geschafft und genau der richtige Moment, um die nächsten Schritte zu planen 😉

Wenn ihr also Lust habt, eure Lernreise weiterzuführen, haben wir jetzt etwas Neues, speziell für unsere deutschsprachige Community:

👉 Unsere neuen deutschsprachigen Live Sessions starten Mitte Juli!

Freut euch auf verschiedene Themen rund um SAP BTP, ideal, um gezielt tiefer in einzelne Bereiche einzusteigen.

Hier ein erster Überblick:

  • Einführung in SAP BTP für Einsteiger
  • Einführung in die SAP Integration Suite für Einsteiger
  • Joule in ABAP (Durchführung in Deutsch)

Einige Sessions sind bereits offen zur Anmeldung, und für weitere Live Sessions ist die Registrierung ebenfalls schon freigeschaltet:

  • Nummernkreise und Nummernvergabe mit RAP
  • Erstellen eines API Providers und eines API Proxy basierend auf einem On-Premise System
  • SAP Fiori Berechtigungskonzept
  • Einführung in die SAP Analytics Cloud Integration mit SAP S/4HANA

Weitere folgen nach und nach. Es lohnt sich also, regelmäßig vorbeizuschauen. Wer weiß, vielleicht ist genau die nächste Session dabei, die perfekt zu eurem aktuellen Lernziel passt 😉

Sichert euch gerne schon jetzt euren Platz. Ich freue mich, viele von euch dort (wieder) zu sehen!

Viele Grüße,
Isabella

entry.title: Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator
entry.content:
Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator

Introduction

In SAP integration projects, technical design documents are critical for capturing interface details, design decisions, mapping rules, adapter configurations, error handling, and operational monitoring. However, creating these documents manually for every SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CPI, interface can be time-consuming and repetitive.

This blog explains a practical use case for building an AI-powered application that can generate SAP CPI technical design documents automatically using inputs such as manual interface details, iFlow packages, and mapping sheets.

The goal is not to replace solution architects or developers, but to accelerate documentation creation and provide a consistent first draft that can be reviewed, edited, and approved by the project team.

Business Problem

SAP CPI developers often spend significant effort documenting integration flows after development is complete. A typical technical design document includes:

  • Interface overview
  • Source and target system details
  • Adapter configuration
  • Mapping and transformation logic
  • Error handling
  • Retry and reprocessing strategy
  • Monitoring and logging
  • Security and authentication
  • Testing scope
  • Deployment checklist

In large integration programs, the same documentation structure is repeated across many interfaces. This creates an opportunity to use AI to generate a structured draft based on available technical inputs.

Proposed Solution

The proposed solution is an AI-assisted SAP CPI TDD Generator.

The application allows users to enter basic interface details, upload an SAP CPI iFlow package, optionally attach a mapping sheet, and generate a technical design document in Word format.

The generated document can include:

  1. Technical design document content
  2. Architecture diagram
  3. Mapping sheet appendix
  4. Test scenarios
  5. Deployment checklist
  6. Assumptions and dependencies

The user can review and edit the generated output before downloading the final document.

High-Level Architecture

The application consists of the following components:

 

Key Features

  1. Manual TDD Generation

The user can provide basic information such as:

  • Interface name
  • Source system
  • Target system
  • Sender adapter
  • Receiver adapter
  • Mapping logic
  • Error handling
  • Integration flow description

The AI model uses this information to generate a structured technical design document.

  1. TDD Generation Using iFlow Package

The application can accept an exported SAP CPI iFlow package in ZIP format. The backend extracts relevant files such as:

  • .iflw
  • XML files
  • Groovy scripts
  • XSLT mappings
  • WSDL files
  • XSD files
  • Properties files

This extracted information is passed to the AI model to generate a more accurate document.

  1. Mapping Sheet Attachment

The user can upload a mapping sheet in Excel or CSV format. The application reads the mapping data and includes it in the generated Word document as an appendix.

This is useful for documenting source-to-target field-level transformations.

  1. Architecture Diagram Generation

The application generates a basic architecture diagram showing the integration flow:

Source System → Sender Adapter → SAP CPI → Mapping/Transformation → Receiver Adapter → Target System

It also includes monitoring, logging, and error handling as part of the diagram.

  1. Word Document Export

The final output is generated as a Word document. The document includes:

  • Interface details
  • Architecture diagram
  • AI-generated TDD content
  • Mapping sheet appendix

This makes the document easy to review, modify, and share with project stakeholders.

 

Technology Stack

A simple prototype can be built using the following technologies:

Component

Technology

Frontend

Streamlit

Backend

Python

AI Model Access

OpenRouter / OpenAI-compatible API

Document Generation

python-docx

Mapping Sheet Processing

pandas, openpyxl

Diagram Generation

Pillow

File Processing

zipfile

This stack is lightweight and suitable for a quick proof of concept.

Sample User Flow

  1. User opens the Streamlit application.
  2. User selects one of the generation options:
    • Manual Entry
    • Generate using iFlow Package
    • Generate using iFlow Package + Mapping Sheet
  3. User provides interface details.
  4. User uploads the iFlow ZIP file.
  5. User uploads the mapping sheet, if available.
  6. User clicks Generate TDD.
  7. The application extracts technical details.
  8. The AI model generates the TDD content.
  9. The user reviews or edits the generated document.
  10. The user downloads the final Word document.

Prompt Engineering Approach

The application uses a controlled prompt to reduce hallucination. The prompt instructs the AI model to behave as an SAP CPI Integration Architect and generate a technical design document using only the provided information.

Example instruction:

Use the iFlow package details where available.

Use the mapping sheet details where available.

Only use information provided by the user, iFlow package, or mapping sheet.

Do not invent missing information.

If any information is missing, mention "To be confirmed".

Do not include passwords, secrets, API keys, or credentials.

This approach helps ensure that the generated document remains reliable and does not include unsupported assumptions.

Generated TDD Sections

The generated technical design document can include the following sections:

  1. Document Purpose
  2. Interface Overview
  3. Business Requirement Summary
  4. Architecture Diagram Description
  5. Source System Details
  6. Target System Details
  7. SAP CPI iFlow Design
  8. Sender Adapter Configuration
  9. Receiver Adapter Configuration
  10. Message Mapping and Transformation Logic
  11. Routing Logic
  12. Exception Handling
  13. Retry and Reprocessing Strategy
  14. Security and Authentication
  15. Monitoring and Logging
  16. Externalized Parameters
  17. Mapping Sheet Summary
  18. Assumptions
  19. Dependencies
  20. Testing Scope
  21. Test Scenarios
  22. Deployment Checklist
  23. Appendix Reference

Benefits

This solution can help integration teams by:

  • Reducing manual documentation effort
  • Improving consistency across TDD documents
  • Accelerating handover from development to support
  • Creating a reusable documentation framework
  • Helping junior developers understand CPI interface design
  • Reducing missing sections in technical documentation
  • Enabling faster review cycles

Important Considerations

While AI can accelerate documentation, generated content should always be reviewed by a developer, solution architect, or technical lead.

The application should not expose or store sensitive information such as:

  • Passwords
  • API keys
  • Client secrets
  • Private certificates
  • Production credentials

For enterprise usage, additional controls should be added, such as:

  • Authentication and authorization
  • Audit logging
  • Secure file handling
  • Data masking
  • Approved AI model usage
  • Document versioning
  • Human approval workflow

Possible Enhancements

Future enhancements can include:

  • Direct connection to SAP Integration Suite APIs
  • Automatic extraction of adapter properties
  • Advanced iFlow XML parsing
  • Interface version comparison
  • Confluence integration
  • Jira integration
  • SAP BTP deployment
  • Approval workflow
  • Reusable client-specific TDD templates
  • Automated test case generation
  • Compliance checks against integration design standards

Conclusion

An AI-powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator can significantly improve productivity in SAP integration projects. By combining SAP CPI artifact extraction, mapping sheet analysis, AI-generated documentation, architecture diagram generation, and Word export, teams can create high-quality first drafts of technical design documents in minutes.

The key principle is to use AI as an accelerator, not as the final authority. The best approach is:

Extract facts → Generate draft → Review and edit → Approve and publish

This use case demonstrates how AI can be practically applied in SAP Integration Suite projects to reduce repetitive effort and improve documentation quality.

Source Code

GIT Repository : https://github.com/jacksnh/sap-cpi-ai-tdd-generator

entry.title: Beyond Basic AI Error Handling in SAP CPI - A Rules-First Architecture with HANA Cloud Learning
entry.content:
The Problem Every Integration Developer Knows

Every SAP CPI developer has lived this scenario.

An iFlow fails in production at an inconvenient hour. You open the MPL log, stare at a cryptic stack trace, and spend the next 30–45 minutes manually cross-referencing adapter configurations, checking credentials, and testing payloads. By the time you identify the root cause  a null field in a mapping, an expired OAuth token, a malformed XML payload  the fix takes five minutes. The diagnosis took an hour.

Recent SAP Community posts have explored using the AI Adapter to automate this diagnosis, and the concept is genuinely sound. But the most common approach  sending the raw exception directly to an LLM has five problems that make it unsuitable for real production environments:

ProblemReal Impact
Raw payload sent to LLMBusiness data leaves the tenant — GDPR risk
Every error hits the LLMAuth failures and timeouts: unnecessary API cost at scale
No deduplicationSame error 1000 times = 1000 API calls
Free-text responseCannot be routed, filtered, or acted on programmatically
Response goes back to senderDevelopers never see the diagnosis

This post describes a different architecture — one that classifies errors deterministically first, sanitizes all business data before any AI call, caches intelligence in SAP HANA Cloud, and uses AI only for errors that are genuinely novel.

In my testing: 3 of 4 error types never touched the LLM at all.

What I Built: The ErrorAnalyzer Agent

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent is a native SAP CPI package  two iFlows, 13 Groovy scripts, a HANA Cloud free-tier instance, and the SAP AI Adapter configured with a breakthrough setting that is completely undocumented in SAP's official documentation.

Package contents:

  • EA_TestHarness — the main analyser iFlow, triggered via ProcessDirect
  • EA_VictimFlow — an example source iFlow that deliberately triggers errors to demonstrate the full pipeline

Technologies used:

  • SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)  iFlows, Groovy scripts, JDBC adapter, AI Adapter, Mail adapter
  • SAP HANA Cloud  free tier, with NLP vector engine enabled for V2
  • OpenRouter  LLM gateway (free tier models available)
  • OpenAI  via OpenRouter for AI diagnosis

Architecture Overview

The diagram below shows the complete three-zone architecture. Zone 1 handles detection inside SAP CPI. Zone 2 contains the intelligence layer — classification, sanitization, and dedup logic. Zone 3 is the AI and persistence layer.

 

 

The key design principle: rules first, AI only when necessary. Known errors (auth failures, timeouts, malformed payloads) are handled deterministically in milliseconds with zero LLM cost. Only genuinely novel or complex errors reach Claude.

Prerequisites

Before building, ensure you have the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite trial or production tenant with Cloud Integration capability enabled
  • OpenRouter account  free tier available at openrouter.ai (no credit card required for free models)
  • SAP HANA Cloud free tier instance provisioned on BTP (covered in setup steps below)
  • Gmail or SMTP account for email notifications
  • Postman or any REST client for testing

Step 1 — Security Material Setup

Before configuring any adapter, create two security artifacts in CPI Monitor.

1a. OpenRouter API Key

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → Secure Parameter

Field Value

NameOpenRouter_API_Key
TypeSecure Parameter
ValueBearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxxxx    ← include "Bearer " prefix — this is critical

Click Deploy. Wait for status to show Deployed  before proceeding.

Important: Do not store only the API key. Store the complete value including the "Bearer " prefix with a space. The AI Adapter uses this as the entire Authorization header value. This is part of the undocumented configuration covered in detail in Step 4.

 

1b. HANA Cloud User Credentials

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → User Credentials

Field Value

NameHANA_CLOUD_DB_USER
TypeUser Credentials
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour HANA instance DBADMIN password

Step 2 — HANA Cloud Setup

2a. Provision the HANA Cloud Instance

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → SAP HANA Cloud → select hana-free plan. Open HANA Cloud Central → Create Instance → SAP HANA Database. Enable the Natural Language Processing (NLP) feature in Advanced Settings  this activates the vector engine for V2 semantic search.

Free tier note: The instance stops every evening automatically and is deleted after 30 days of inactivity. Restart it manually when needed from HANA Cloud Central.

2b. Configure JDBC Data Source in CPI

Navigate to: Monitor → Manage JDBC Material → JDBC Data Source tab → Add

Field Value

NameHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Database TypeSAP HANA Cloud
JDBC URLjdbc:sap://<your-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443?encrypt=true&validateCertificate=true
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour DBADMIN password
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

Click Deploy → Test Connection. You should see a green success confirmation before proceeding.

 

2c. Create the Database Tables

Open HANA Cloud Central → SQL Console → connect using DBADMIN credentials → run the following SQL:

CREATE TABLE ERROR_PATTERNS (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    EXCEPTION_CLASS  NVARCHAR(255),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    ENVIRONMENT      NVARCHAR(20),
    AI_SUMMARY       NVARCHAR(500),
    ROOT_CAUSE       NVARCHAR(1000),
    RESOLUTION_STEPS NCLOB,
    SEVERITY         NVARCHAR(10),
    AUTO_RECOVERABLE NVARCHAR(5),
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    LAST_SEEN        TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FIRST_SEEN       TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    MPL_ID           NVARCHAR(100),
    RESOLVED         NVARCHAR(5)     DEFAULT 'false'
);

CREATE TABLE ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    LAST_ANALYZED    TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    CACHED_ANALYSIS  NCLOB
);

Step 3 — Build the EA_TestHarness iFlow

Create a new Integration Flow in your package. The complete flow structure is shown below.

 

 

The flow has the following step sequence:

  1. ProcessDirect Sender — address: /erroranalyzer/triage
  2. Content Modifier (setParams) — sets iflowName, environment, llmModel as Exchange Properties
  3. Classifier — Groovy script, categorises the error
  4. Sanitizer — Groovy script, masks payload values and generates fingerprint
  5. Router 2 — routes Non-LLM vs LLM-eligible errors
  6. DedupCheck — Groovy script, builds SELECT SQL for HANA lookup
  7. JDBC_SELECT Request Reply — executes SELECT against ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  8. ProcessDedupResult — Groovy script, parses JDBC XML response
  9. Router 3 — routes cache hit vs cache miss
  10. BuildCachedResponse / BuildPrompt — builds response from cache or builds LLM request
  11. Request Reply → AI Adapter — calls Claude via OpenRouter
  12. ParseLLMResponse → BuildEnhancedResponse — structures the AI analysis
  13. BuildEmailBody → Mail Receiver — sends HTML alert email
  14. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  15. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

Content Modifier — setParams configuration

Add a Content Modifier as the first step after Start. In the Exchange Property tab, add:

Property Name Type Value

   
environmentConstantDEV
llmModelConstantopenai/gpt-oss-120b:free  ← externalized, change without redeployment

Step 4 — The Classifier Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Classifier. This is the core of the rules-first approach — pure pattern matching, zero API cost, millisecond response.

Category Trigger Action Hits LLM

CONNECTIVITYConnectException, timeout, connection refusedRETRY_BACKOFFNo
AUTH_FAILUREHTTP 401/403, unauthorized, forbiddenREFRESH_CREDENTIALSNo
PAYLOAD_MALFORMEDSAXParseException, malformed XMLDEAD_LETTERNo
MAPPING_FAILUREMappingException, cannot convert, XSLT errorANALYZEYes
UNKNOWNEverything elseANALYZEYes
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    String exClass = (json.exceptionClass ?: "").toString()
    String exMsg   = (json.exceptionMessage ?: "").toString()

    String category; String action; boolean needsLLM = false

    if (exClass.contains("ConnectException") ||
        exMsg =~ /(?i)(timed out|timeout|connection refused|no route to host)/) {
        category = "CONNECTIVITY"; action = "RETRY_BACKOFF"
    } else if (exMsg =~ /(?i)statusCode:\s*40[13]/ ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(unauthorized|forbidden|invalid credentials)/) {
        category = "AUTH_FAILURE"; action = "REFRESH_CREDENTIALS"
    } else if (exClass.contains("SAXParse") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(content is not allowed in prolog|malformed)/) {
        category = "PAYLOAD_MALFORMED"; action = "DEAD_LETTER"
    } else if (exClass.contains("Mapping") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(xslt|mapping|cannot convert)/) {
        category = "MAPPING_FAILURE"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    } else {
        category = "UNKNOWN"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    }

    message.setProperty("errorCategory",    category)
    message.setProperty("suggestedAction",  action)
    message.setProperty("needsLLM",         needsLLM.toString())
    message.setProperty("exceptionClass",   exClass)
    message.setProperty("exceptionMessage", exMsg)
    message.setProperty("rawPayload",       (json.payload ?: "").toString())
    if (json.mplId)       message.setProperty("mplId",       json.mplId.toString())
    if (json.iflowName)   message.setProperty("iflowName",   json.iflowName.toString())
    if (json.environment) message.setProperty("environment", json.environment.toString())
    return message
}

Step 5 — The Sanitizer Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Sanitizer. This script parses the raw payload — XML or JSON — and replaces every leaf value with a typed token before anything leaves the tenant.

A customer record like this:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "ORD-001",
    "amount": null,
    "customer": "John Smith",
    "taxId": "DE123456789"
  }
}

Becomes this — safe to send to any external LLM:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "[STRING:7]",
    "amount": "[EMPTY]",
    "customer": "[STRING:10]",
    "taxId": "[STRING:11]"
  }
}

The Sanitizer also generates a fingerprint: MD5(iFlowName + exceptionClass + errorCategory). Including iFlowName ensures that the same MappingException in two different iFlows produces different fingerprints — preventing wrong cross-iFlow cache hits.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlParser
import groovy.util.XmlNodePrinter
import groovy.util.Node
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import java.security.MessageDigest

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String raw = ((message.getProperty("rawPayload") ?: "").toString()).trim()
    String sanitized
    try {
        if (raw.startsWith("<")) {
            def root = new XmlParser().parseText(raw)
            maskXmlNode(root)
            def sw = new StringWriter()
            new XmlNodePrinter(new PrintWriter(sw)).print(root)
            sanitized = sw.toString().trim()
        } else if (raw.startsWith("{") || raw.startsWith("[")) {
            def obj = new JsonSlurper().parseText(raw)
            sanitized = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(maskJson(obj)))
        } else if (raw.isEmpty()) {
            sanitized = "[EMPTY]"
        } else {
            sanitized = maskValue(raw)
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        sanitized = "[UNPARSEABLE: ${e.message}]"
    }
    String fpSource = (message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "").toString()
    message.setProperty("sanitizedPayload", sanitized)
    message.setProperty("fingerprint", md5(fpSource))
    return message
}

void maskXmlNode(Node node) {
    if (node.children().any { it instanceof Node }) {
        node.children().each { if (it instanceof Node) maskXmlNode(it) }
    } else { node.setValue(maskValue(node.text())) }
}

def maskJson(obj) {
    if (obj instanceof Map) { def m = [:]; obj.each { k, v -> m[k] = maskJson(v) }; return m }
    else if (obj instanceof List) { return obj.collect { maskJson(it) } }
    else { return maskValue(obj == null ? "" : obj.toString()) }
}

String maskValue(String v) {
    if (v == null || v.trim().isEmpty()) return "[EMPTY]"
    v = v.trim()
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+/)                return "[INT:${v.length()}]"
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+\.\d+/)           return "[DECIMAL]"
    if (v ==~ /\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}.*/) return "[DATE]"
    if (v ==~ /(?i)(true|false)/)     return "[BOOL]"
    return "[STRING:${v.length()}]"
}

String md5(String s) {
    MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5").digest(s.bytes).encodeHex().toString()
}

Step 6 — Router Configuration

Add a Router step after the Sanitizer. Configure two routes:

Router 2 — routes based on whether LLM is needed:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (LLM path)${property.needsLLM} = 'true'Non-XML
Non_LLM (default)Default Route — checkedN/A

Router 3 — routes based on HANA cache result:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (cache hit)${property.cacheHit} = 'true'Non-XML
Default (cache miss)Default Route — checkedN/A

 

 

 

Step 7 — HANA Dedup Check via JDBC

On the LLM path, before calling Claude, the system checks HANA Cloud. Add these three steps in sequence:

  1. DedupCheck — Groovy script that builds the SELECT SQL
  2. Request Reply → JDBC Receiver — executes the query against HANA
  3. ProcessDedupResult — parses the XML response from JDBC

Important: CPI's JDBC adapter returns query results as XML — not JSON. The structure is ROOT → select_response → row. Use groovy.util.XmlSlurper (not groovy.xml.XmlSlurper — that class does not exist in CPI's Groovy runtime).

// ProcessDedupResult — parse JDBC XML response
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    try {
        def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(body)
        def rows = xml.select_response.row
        if (rows && rows.size() > 0) {
            def row = rows[0]
            String cachedAnalysis = row.CACHED_ANALYSIS.text()
            String occCount = row.OCCURRENCE_COUNT.text() ?: "1"
            if (cachedAnalysis) {
                message.setProperty("cacheHit", "true")
                message.setProperty("cachedAnalysis", cachedAnalysis)
                message.setProperty("cacheOccurrenceCount",
                    (occCount.toInteger() + 1).toString())
            } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
        } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    } catch(Exception e) { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    return message
}

JDBC Receiver Adapter configuration for both SELECT and WRITE steps:

Field Value

JDBC Data Source AliasHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

 

Step 8 — The Undocumented AI Adapter Configuration

This section is the most important technical finding in this post.

If you have tried connecting SAP's AI Adapter (Custom-OpenAI-REST) to OpenRouter or any external LLM endpoint, you have almost certainly encountered this:

401 Unauthorized — Missing Authentication Header

It does not matter how many times you redeploy the Secure Parameter. Basic mode simply does not work with OpenRouter. After extensive testing, I identified the exact combination of three settings that resolves this completely.

Fix 1 — Configuration Type: Advanced (not Basic)

In the Processing tab, change Configuration Type from Basic to Advanced. Basic mode uses SAP's internal OpenAI abstraction layer which is incompatible with OpenRouter's authentication format. Advanced mode gives direct REST control.

 

 

 

Fix 2 — Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter

Store the full Authorization value — not just the key — in the Secure Parameter: Bearer sk-or-v1-xxx. The adapter uses this as the complete Authorization header value. Without the prefix, the header is malformed and the request fails.

Fix 3 — Split the URL across tabs

Connection tab and Processing tab must share the URL:

  • Connection tab Address: https://openrouter.ai
  • Processing tab Relative URL: /api/v1/chat/completions

 

Complete Working Configuration

Connection Tab:

Field Value

Message ProtocolCustom-OpenAI-REST
Addresshttps://openrouter.ai
API Key AliasOpenRouter_API_Key
Connection Timeout60000 ms
Response Timeout60000 ms

 

Processing Tab:

Field Value

Configuration TypeAdvanced ← Critical — Basic fails with 401
HTTP MethodPOST
Relative URL/api/v1/chat/completions
Request Payload SourceExchange Body
Request HeadersHTTP-Referer|X-Title|Content-Type

 

Step 9 — Build the LLM Request (BuildPrompt)

The BuildPrompt Groovy script assembles the request body for Claude. It reads the sanitized context from message properties — never from the raw payload — and forces a structured JSON response format.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String errorCategory = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "UNKNOWN"
    String exClass       = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String exMsg         = message.getProperty("exceptionMessage") ?: ""
    String sanitized     = message.getProperty("sanitizedPayload") ?: "[EMPTY]"
    String iflowName     = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown"
    String environment   = message.getProperty("environment") ?: "Unknown"
    String llmModel      = message.getProperty("llmModel") ?: "anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku"

    String userContent = """\
Analyze this SAP CPI runtime error and respond with ONLY valid JSON.

Required structure:
{
  "error_summary": "one clear sentence",
  "root_cause": "specific technical cause",
  "resolution_steps": ["step 1", "step 2", "step 3"],
  "auto_recoverable": false,
  "severity": "HIGH"
}

Severity: HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW only.
auto_recoverable: boolean only.

Error details:
iFlow: ${iflowName}
Environment: ${environment}
Category: ${errorCategory}
Exception class: ${exClass}
Exception message: ${exMsg}
Sanitized payload structure (real values masked):
${sanitized}"""

    def requestBody = [
        model: llmModel,
        messages: [
            [role: "system", content: "You are an SAP CPI integration expert. Respond only with valid JSON."],
            [role: "user",   content: userContent]
        ],
        temperature: 0.2,
        max_tokens: 400
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(requestBody))
    return message
}

Step 10 — EA_VictimFlow: Wiring the Exception Subprocess

EA_VictimFlow demonstrates how to connect any existing iFlow to the ErrorAnalyzer. The pattern is reusable — copy the exception subprocess to any failing iFlow by changing only the iflowName constant.

 

Add a Content Modifier at the start of EA_VictimFlow with these Exchange Properties:

Property Name Type Value

iflowNameConstantEA_VictimFlow
environmentConstantDEV

Inside the Exception Subprocess, add a Groovy script named ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def exCaught   = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")
    String exClass = exCaught ? exCaught.getClass().getName() : "Unknown"
    String exMsg   = exCaught ? exCaught.getMessage() : "Unknown error"
    String mplId   = message.getHeader("SAP_MessageProcessingLogID", String.class) ?: "unavailable"
    String iflowName = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown_iFlow"
    String payload = ""
    try { payload = message.getBody(String) ?: "" } catch(Exception e) { payload = "" }

    def envelope = [
        exceptionClass:   exClass,
        exceptionMessage: exMsg,
        payload:          payload,
        mplId:            mplId,
        iflowName:        iflowName,
        environment:      message.getProperty("environment") ?: "DEV"
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(envelope))
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")
    return message
}

Connect the ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder to a ProcessDirect Receiver with address: /erroranalyzer/triage

Important: SAP_ApplicationID is NOT the iFlow name — it is the application binding ID. Always use a Content Modifier constant for iflowName as shown above.

Step 10b — Writing to HANA Cloud

After Claude returns the analysis, two separate JDBC write steps persist the intelligence to HANA. They are intentionally split into two steps because CPI's JDBC adapter executes exactly one SQL statement per call — chaining two statements with a semicolon throws a syntax error.

PrepareHANAWrite — writes full analysis to ERROR_PATTERNS

This script builds a MERGE statement that either inserts a new error record or increments the occurrence count if the fingerprint already exists. Note the HANA-specific syntax: WITH PRIMARY KEY is required — standard WHERE syntax fails with a syntax error.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llm = new JsonSlurper().parseText(
                  message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}")

    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")    ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory")  ?: ""
    String exClass     = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")      ?: ""
    String environment = message.getProperty("environment")    ?: ""
    String mplId       = message.getProperty("mplId")          ?: ""
    String aiSummary   = (llm.error_summary   ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String rootCause   = (llm.root_cause       ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String steps       = JsonOutput.toJson(llm.resolution_steps ?: []).replace("'","''")
    String severity    = (llm.severity         ?: "MEDIUM").toString()
    String autoRec     = (llm.auto_recoverable ?: false).toString()
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_PATTERNS
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, EXCEPTION_CLASS, IFLOW_NAME,
         ENVIRONMENT, AI_SUMMARY, ROOT_CAUSE, RESOLUTION_STEPS,
         SEVERITY, AUTO_RECOVERABLE, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
         LAST_SEEN, FIRST_SEEN, MPL_ID, RESOLVED)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${exClass}',
                '${iflowName}','${environment}','${aiSummary}',
                '${rootCause}','${steps}','${severity}',
                '${autoRec}', 1,'${now}','${now}','${mplId}','false')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

PrepareDedup — writes fast lookup entry to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llmRaw         = message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}"
    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")   ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")     ?: ""
    String cached      = llmRaw.replace("'","''")
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, IFLOW_NAME,
         LAST_ANALYZED, OCCURRENCE_COUNT, CACHED_ANALYSIS)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${iflowName}',
                '${now}', 1,'${cached}')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

The complete write sequence after Claude analysis:

  1. BuildEnhancedResponse — assembles the final JSON response
  2. BuildEmailBody — builds the HTML alert email
  3. Mail Receiver — dispatches the email notification
  4. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  5. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  6. End

Both JDBC_WRITE and JDBC_WRITE2 use the same receiver adapter configuration as JDBC_SELECT  Data Source Alias: HANA_Cloud_JDBC, timeouts: 30 seconds.

Email is dispatched before the HANA writes so that if HANA is unavailable (free tier stops nightly), the developer notification still goes out regardless.

Step 11 — Testing

Deploy both iFlows. Test using any REST client by POSTing to the EA_VictimFlow HTTPS endpoint with plain text body:

Request Body Error Category Expected Behaviour

mappingMAPPING_FAILUREHits Claude, writes to HANA, sends email with AI analysis
authAUTH_FAILURENo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
timeoutCONNECTIVITYNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
xmlPAYLOAD_MALFORMEDNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email

Results

First Run — MAPPING_FAILURE (new error, hits Claude)

{
  "fingerprint":     "dfb60537dd72852be164ee7f46bab45a",
  "mplId":           "AGoi2tHxrNFx-9CloVUzH46UcB-6",
  "iflowName":       "EA_VictimFlow",
  "environment":     "DEV",
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "suggestedAction": "ANALYZE",
  "sanitizedPayload": "{\"order\":{\"id\":\"[STRING:7]\",\"amount\":\"[EMPTY]\"}}",
  "servedFromCache": "false",
  "llmParseSuccess": "true",
  "aiAnalysis": {
    "summary":   "Mapping failed because null Amount field cannot be converted to decimal",
    "rootCause": "Source system sent null where decimal value was expected",
